title: Coding With OpenAI: You Must Ask The Right Questions
author: Mike LaPeter
text:
OpenAI Codex: You Must Ask The Right Questions
So far from what I can tell, 2 things happen when you first experience OpenAI’s GPT-3:
- Your mind is completely blown. This is incredible! This could do ANYTHING.
- This could, this could… well, crap. Suddenly, your mind is completely empty.
You try a few different inputs, but then you don’t quite get the results you want. So you wander off. You think maybe it’s not you, it’s GPT-3. It’s not ready for a relationship.
But real relationships take work. This, then, is the start of mine.
My ultimate plan is to build simple, well-made tools to assist neurodivergent programmers who understand things on a high level but often struggle recalling basic syntax. I’ve struggled with this personally my entire life: my goldfish-grade memory is so bad I’ve forgotten my own birthday before. I still copy and paste the github instructions every time I start a new repo.
So where to start? In my case, I’m going to explore building a small project from scratch to get a sense of the strengths and weaknesses of OpenAi’s Codex, their newest AI model designed specifically for code.
My project is something simple that I’ve never quite gotten around to: a choose your own adventure game called Ronnie Regret. It’s like a regular choose your own adventure, except no matter what choice you make you end up screwed.
Let’s make it browser based in the style of a dark room, and let’s start by using the OpenAI Codex JavaScript Sandbox (in closed beta as of this writing, signup here):
Not bad! A little eager about making out, perhaps, but as long as we don’t arouse sexual excitement we should be okay. We’re replacing all the narrative text with our own, anyway — we’re just here for the code. The real crime here is that it keeps just overwriting our ronnie
variable, which I’ll have to fix manually, along with a couple other minor errors.
But honestly — I’m already pretty impressed. The hardest part for me in any project is just getting to those first lines of code: picking a framework, looking at various styling templates… basically analysis paralysis. It feels super refreshing to just dive in. And it already feels like a real thing:
Let’s spruce it up real quick using our new powers. Comments are mine, code is from Codex:
/* set the font to roboto slab */
document.body.style.fontFamily = 'Roboto Slab, serif';/* center everything */
var center = document.createElement('div');
center.style.position = 'absolute';
center.style.top = '50%';
center.style.left = '50%';
center.style.transform = 'translate(-50%, -50%)';
document.body.appendChild(center);
Again cleanup needed, I had to replace center
with ronnie
to match my earlier edits. But looking better:
At this point, as I thought about what to ask Codex to make some nice buttons, I started to run into my own limitations of using the web based sandbox. It’s insanely powerful by itself (just take a look at the OpenAI Codex Live Demo) but for a noob like me it felt trickier to use as my project grew.
That, and I had already installed Github Copilot (thanks GitHub!) as an extension in VS code, and it was starting to steal the show. So at this point, I switched. Luckily, it’s also powered by OpenAI Codex, so no AI feelings were hurt in the process.
